title: Kickoff Brief — intraclass: Modern Intraclass Correlation Coefficients in R
text: Build R package operationalizes generalizability-theory (GT) framework intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) developed ten Hove, Jorgensen & van der Ark, using modern variance-component estimation rather classical ANOVA / McGraw–Wong mean-squares approach. package exist (differentiators): - Existing tools split two camps. classical ones (psych, irr, irrNA, irrICC, ICCDesign) ANOVA / mean-squares based. assume balanced data, though tolerate missing cells — irrICC (Gwet) irrNA compute ICCs wide rating matrix NAs, closest prior art incomplete designs, still within classical model rather mixed-model / MC-CI approach. model-based ones (performance::icc, gtheory, misty) extract variance-partition coefficient fitted model implement full interrater-reliability ICC family (absolute agreement vs. consistency × single vs. average × fixed vs. random raters), error-variance framing, selection framework. - GT framework’s reference code loose supplementary scripts, engineered package. - gap fill: coherent, well-tested package (1) fits variance components modern engines, (2) computes correct ICC stated design proper interval estimation, (3) handles imbalanced / incomplete / multilevel designs, (4) teaches user ICC choose . Audience: applied researchers psychology, education, medicine, behavioral science need defensible IRR estimates guidance. first-class goal, afterthought: package pkgdown site place learn ICC best practices. Every estimator’s docs every vignette must explain estimand, assumptions, tradeoffs behind “knob,” guide reader sensible choice. Estimation engines (dependency discipline): - Default engine: lme4 /glmmTMB (MLE random-effects models). framework’s 2025 simulation work favors MLE-RE Monte-Carlo CIs feasibility accuracy, spine. - Optional engines (Suggests, never Imports): brms / rstanarm (Bayesian) lavaan / blavaan (SEM / common-factor). Keep base install light fast; gate optional-engine code behind rlang::check_installed().

title: Choosing an ICC
text: ratings six-subject, four-rater example Shrout Fleiss (1979), long, one-row-per-rating format icc() expects. Every subject rated every rater, complete, balanced two-way design – clean case choices easiest see.
code: str(ratings) #> 'data.frame':    24 obs. of  3 variables: #>  $ subject: Factor w/ 6 levels "1","2","3","4",..: 1 2 3 4 5 6 1 2 3 4 ... #>  $ rater  : Factor w/ 4 levels "1","2","3","4": 1 1 1 1 1 1 2 2 2 2 ... #>  $ score  : num  9 6 8 7 10 6 2 1 4 1 ...

what: A prior question: are the raters crossed? (model)
title: Choosing an ICC
text: every subject rated set raters, different set time? raters judge everyone (crossed, two-way design, like ratings), keep default model = "twoway" four choices apply. instead subject rated whichever raters happened available – “rater 1” one subject nothing “rater 1” another – raters interchangeable design one-way (model = "oneway"). one-way model separate systematic rater differences error, folds residual reports single ICC(1) / ICC(k). makes conservative coefficient: data ICC(1) 0.17, two-way ICC(,1) ICC(C,1) see next, precisely separate rater effect one-way absorbs. agreement/consistency fixed/random choices apply one-way (rater term reason ) – answer question first.
code: oneway <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, model = "oneway", seed = 2024) oneway #> # Intraclass correlation: one-way random #> Subjects: 6 | Ratings: 24 (4 per subject, balanced) | raters interchangeable #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(1)      0.166   [0.008, 0.838] #>   ICC(k)      0.443   [0.032, 0.954] #> Variance components: subject 1.244, residual 6.264 (rater confounded) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(1) = ICC(1,1), ICC(k) = ICC(1,k)

title: Choosing an ICC
text: actual value need match, rank order? Absolute agreement treats systematic difference raters – one judge scoring consistently higher another – error. Consistency forgives constant per-rater offset asks whether raters rank subjects way. ICC(,1) 0.29 ICC(C,1) 0.71. gap noise – direct read-much raters differ average level. Consistency never smaller agreement, drops source error. Choose agreement number must trusted (clinical score, physical measurement) consistency relative standing matters (ranking applicants).
code: agreement <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, type = "agreement", seed = 2024) agreement #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 6 | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 24 of 24 cells (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(A,1)    0.290   [0.050, 0.711] #>   ICC(A,k)    0.620   [0.173, 0.908] #> Variance components: subject 2.556, rater 5.244, residual 1.019 #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1), ICC(A,k) = ICC(2,k)  consistency <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, type = "consistency", seed = 2024) consistency #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, consistency #> Subjects: 6 | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 24 of 24 cells (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(C,1)    0.715   [0.340, 0.926] #>   ICC(C,k)    0.909   [0.673, 0.980] #> Variance components: subject 2.556, rater 5.244, residual 1.019

title: Choosing an ICC
text: use one rater’s score, mean several? icc() returns rows default: ICC(*,1) reliability single rater, ICC(*,k) reliability mean k raters. Averaging cancels independent error, ICC(*,k) always larger number (Spearman–Brown relationship). absolute agreement , single-rater 0.29 rises 0.62 four-rater mean. Report ICC(*,k) averaged score actually act ; downstream users see one rater’s judgment, ICC(*,1) honest figure. Request one unit = "single", unit = "average", default c("single", "average").

title: Choosing an ICC
text: raters sample want generalize beyond, entire population interest? Random raters (default) sample; coefficient generalizes rater universe drawn . Fixed raters judges care , coefficient generalize past . icc() warns raters = "fixed" random recommended default interrater reliability: fixing raters answers narrower question. balanced design fixed random point estimates coincide, fit different models intervals differ – incomplete data even point estimates diverge (see ). Prefer random unless truly never intend generalize beyond exact raters.
code: fixed <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, raters = "fixed", seed = 2024) #> Warning: Modeling raters as fixed restricts inference to exactly these raters; you #> cannot generalize to other raters. #> ℹ For interrater reliability, the two-way random model (`raters = "random"`) is #>   the recommended default (ten Hove et al. 2024; McGraw & Wong 1996, Case 2). #> ℹ Use "fixed" only when these are the entire population of raters you will ever #>   use.

what: A fifth choice: subject- vs. cluster-level
title: Choosing an ICC
text: Everything takes subject object measurement. subjects nested higher-level units – pupils within classrooms, patients within clinics – may instead want reliability cluster mean, multilevel ICC (ten Hove, Jorgensen & van der Ark, 2022). Pass cluster column icc() reports subject-level (within-cluster) cluster-level (-cluster) coefficients side side. Multilevel designs article works full example.

title: Choosing an ICC

code: choose_icc(type = "agreement", unit = "single", raters = "random") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Recommendation: ICC(A,1) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Absolute agreement: the value itself must match; a systematic difference between raters counts as error. #>   - Single rater: you will act on one rater's score. #>   - Random raters: a sample you generalize beyond, to the rater universe they were drawn from. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, unit = "single") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). what: Within-cell replicates: interaction vs. pure error
title: D-studies and within-cell replicates
text: far every subject--rater cell holds single rating. rater rates subject — within-cell replicates — can separate two things single rating confounds: subject--rater interaction (rater systematically score particular subject high low — stable disagreement?) pure error (much rater’s repeat ratings subject wobble). icc() detects replicates fits interaction model automatically: variance-components line now shows subject:rater (interaction) residual (pure error) separate terms. single-occasion rows (occasions = 1) ordinary ICCs — single rating’s error still includes interaction — now fit correctly rather folding interaction residual. occasion-averaged rows (occasions = 3 ) give reliability rater’s mean three ratings: averaging cuts pure error (interaction), coefficients higher. Within-cell replicates extend beyond balanced two-way random example: fixed raters (balanced), multilevel designs (crossed Design 1 nested Design 2, balanced), ragged replicates single occasion supported. remains open occasion-averaged coefficient ragged replicates — unequal per-cell counts single effective-occasion divisor validated oracle — along compound fixed--ragged multilevel--ragged corners.
code: set.seed(2025) ns <- 20 nr <- 4 no <- 3 grid <- expand.grid(subject = seq_len(ns), rater = seq_len(nr), occ = seq_len(no)) subj <- rnorm(ns, sd = 1.1)[grid$subject] rater <- rnorm(nr, sd = 0.8)[grid$rater] sr <- rnorm(ns * nr, sd = 0.6)[(grid$rater - 1) * ns + grid$subject] reps <- data.frame(   subject = factor(grid$subject),   rater = factor(grid$rater),   score = 10 + subj + rater + sr + rnorm(nrow(grid), sd = 0.7) )  icc(reps, score, subject, rater, occasions = c("single", "average")) #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 20 | Raters: 4 (random) | 80 cells x 3 replicates (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     occasions estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(A,1)          1   0.263   [0.082, 0.490] #>   ICC(A,1)          3   0.300   [0.087, 0.564] #>   ICC(A,k)          1   0.588   [0.263, 0.794] #>   ICC(A,k)          3   0.631   [0.277, 0.838] #> Variance components: subject 0.631, rater 0.901, subject:rater 0.428, residual 0.443 #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1), ICC(A,k) = ICC(2,k)

what: The mixed-model engines: glmmTMB and lme4
title: Estimation engines

REML mixed-model fits model, given dataset return coefficients numerical tolerance — choice fitting backend, estimand. two point estimates agree well within rounding, Monte-Carlo intervals coincide 0.01: lme4 interval built parameter covariance supplied merDeriv package, transformed onto boundary-aware log-scale glmmTMB uses. glmmTMB remains recommended default — one required dependency robust variance component sits exactly zero boundary, lme4 route form interval directs back glmmTMB. lme4 otherwise full design parity glmmTMB — fixed-rater every multilevel design, balanced incomplete/ragged data — degrading glmmTMB variance boundary.
code: glmmtmb <- tidy(icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, engine = "glmmTMB", seed = 1)) lme4 <- tidy(icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, engine = "lme4", seed = 1)) data.frame(   index = glmmtmb$index,   glmmTMB = round(glmmtmb$estimate, 4),   lme4 = round(lme4$estimate, 4) ) #>      index glmmTMB   lme4 #> 1 ICC(A,1)  0.2898 0.2898 #> 2 ICC(A,k)  0.6201 0.6201

Unlike lme4, just different backend estimator: matters coefficient ask . Consistency coefficients ratio subject residual variances, SEM returns identically mixed model. Absolute agreement different. Following Jorgensen (2021), rater variance recovered mean structure variance estimated indicator intercepts. indicator-mean estimator genuinely different estimator rater variance mixed model’s random effect: two asymptotically equivalent match conventional generalizability-theory software (GENOVA, gtheory) closely real data [Vispoel et al. 2022], small design differ modest amount — ICC(,1) 0.284 lavaan versus 0.290 mixed model, raw variance four estimated rater means carries small-sample noise mixed model shrinks away. “right”? Neither wrong — two defensible estimators population quantity. Use "glmmTMB" (default) want mixed-model random-rater estimate wider, generalize--new-raters interval; reach "lavaan" working inside SEM generalizability-theory workflow want results comparable literature. One-way multilevel designs still directed mixed-model engines.
code: axes <- expand.grid(   type = c("agreement", "consistency"),   unit = c("single", "average"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) compare <- do.call(rbind, Map(function(type, unit) {   g <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, type = type, unit = unit,            engine = "glmmTMB", seed = 1)   l <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, type = type, unit = unit,            engine = "lavaan", seed = 1)   data.frame(     index = tidy(g)$index,     glmmTMB = round(tidy(g)$estimate, 4),     lavaan = round(tidy(l)$estimate, 4)   ) }, axes$type, axes$unit)) compare[!duplicated(compare$index), ] #>                 index glmmTMB lavaan #> agreement    ICC(A,1)  0.2898 0.2843 #> consistency  ICC(C,1)  0.7148 0.7148 #> agreement1   ICC(A,k)  0.6201 0.6137 #> consistency1 ICC(C,k)  0.9093 0.9093

title: Estimation engines
text: engine = "brms" fits variance components fully Bayesian framework (Stan, via brms package) — approach ten Hove, Jorgensen & van der Ark (2020) developed interrater reliability. Instead single REML point Monte-Carlo interval, samples posterior every variance component reads ICC draws: point estimate posterior mode (MAP) interval credible interval (covered Confidence-interval methods). samples Stan model, engine slower others needs brms package (optional Suggests dependency). examples shown pre-computed output: fitting Stan model needs toolchain available site built, chunks evaluated knit time. header names brms (MCMC) engine posterior credible interval, ci_method = "posterior" automatic. tiny six-subject design MAP ICC(,1) (0.24) sits little glmmTMB REML value (0.29): MAP mode wide, right-skewed posterior, small sample pulls (ten Hove et al. 2020 note small rater counts). Sampler settings — chains, iterations, backend, parallel cores — pass brm_args, e.g. brm_args = list(chains = 4, cores = 4).
code: bayes <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, engine = "brms", seed = 1) bayes #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 6 | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 24 of 24 cells (complete) #> Engine: brms (MCMC) | CI: 95% posterior credible (4000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(A,1)    0.241   [0.066, 0.649] #>   ICC(A,k)    0.679   [0.221, 0.881] #> Variance components: subject 1.522, rater 2.653, residual 0.962 #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1), ICC(A,k) = ICC(2,k)

title: Estimation engines
text: default engine places weakly-informative half-t(4, 0, 1) prior every random-effect standard deviation — sourced prior (ten Hove et al. 2020, §3.3/§4.1) every coverage result package depends . can supply prior prior-sensitivity method-comparison work prior argument (brms prior object), icc() warns loudly, leaving sourced prior voids coverage guarantees poorly chosen SD prior can worsen small-sample boundary behavior: deliberately -tight normal(0, 0.1) prior squeezes every standard deviation toward zero, collapsing ICC nearly nothing — vivid reminder prior load-bearing, casual knob. Leave prior unset unless specifically intend depart sourced default. brms engine covers design family mixed-model engines (two-way random fixed, one-way, multilevel designs), balanced incomplete data.
code: library(brms) icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, engine = "brms",   prior = set_prior("normal(0, 0.1)", class = "sd"), seed = 1) #> Warning message: #> Using a custom `prior` instead of the sourced half-t(4, 0, 1). #> ! This VOIDS the package's coverage guarantees: the credible-interval coverage #>   results (ten Hove et al. 2020) hold only for the sourced prior. #> i A vague or flat SD prior can WORSEN small-`k` boundary bias -- the half-t is #>   weakly informative on purpose (Principle #3's regime). #> i Leave `prior` unset for the sourced default unless you are running #>   prior-sensitivity or method-comparison work.

title: Multilevel designs: subject and cluster level
text: subjects nested clusters, two reliabilities defined: Subject level (within-cluster): reliably raters distinguish subjects within cluster? Cluster level (-cluster): reliably raters distinguish cluster means? Ignoring nesting conflates biases (ten Hove, Jorgensen & van der Ark, 2022). Passing cluster column icc() fits multilevel model reports level separately. Consider pupils nested classrooms, pupil rated panel raters. simulate design substantial classroom-level signal modest within-classroom differences: levels come back one call. cluster-level ICC higher two: raters agree classrooms score high pupils within classroom — exactly pattern expect true variation lives classrooms. number report depends decision make: classroom-level intervention cares cluster-level reliability, pupil-level one subject level. Request just one level = "subject" level = "cluster".
code: set.seed(2025) n_class <- 16 n_pupil <- 5 n_rater <- 4 grid <- expand.grid(   pupil = seq_len(n_pupil),   classroom = seq_len(n_class),   rater = seq_len(n_rater) ) class_effect <- rnorm(n_class, sd = 1.3)[grid$classroom] pupil_effect <- rnorm(n_class * n_pupil, sd = 0.6)[   (grid$classroom - 1) * n_pupil + grid$pupil ] rater_effect <- rnorm(n_rater, sd = 0.4)[grid$rater] school <- data.frame(   classroom = factor(grid$classroom),   pupil = factor(paste(grid$classroom, grid$pupil, sep = "_")),   rater = factor(grid$rater),   score = 10 + class_effect + pupil_effect + rater_effect +     rnorm(nrow(grid), sd = 0.7) ) icc(school, score, subject = pupil, rater = rater, cluster = classroom, seed = 1) #> ℹ Treating raters with the same label in different clusters as the same raters #>   (crossed with clusters, Design 1). #> ℹ If each cluster has its own raters, give them cluster-unique labels or pass #>   `design = "nested_in_clusters"`. #> # Intraclass correlation: multilevel two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 80 in 16 clusters | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 320 (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   level      index     estimate   95% CI #>   subject    ICC(A,1)    0.431   [0.249, 0.561] #>   subject    ICC(A,k)    0.751   [0.571, 0.836] #>   cluster    ICC(A,1)    0.880   [0.000, 0.972] #>   cluster    ICC(A,k)    0.967   [0.000, 0.993] #> Variance components: cluster 0.998, subject 0.461, rater 0.136, cluster:rater 0.000, residual 0.473 #>  #> This message is displayed once per session.

title: Modern Intraclass Correlation Coefficients
text: intraclass computes interrater-reliability intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) within generalizability-theory framework, using modern variance-component estimation (linear mixed models) rather classical ANOVA / mean-squares approach. aims (1) fit variance components modern engines, (2) compute correct ICC stated design proper (boundary-aware Monte-Carlo) interval estimation, (3) handle imbalanced, incomplete, multilevel designs, (4) help decide ICC choose, — docs website place learn ICC best practice, just call functions. [!NOTE] package approaching first release. full interrater-reliability ICC family implemented — two-way (absolute agreement vs. consistency, single vs. average, random vs. fixed raters) one-way designs, imbalanced incomplete (missing-cell) data, multilevel designs (subject vs. cluster level, raters crossed nested clusters/subjects) — boundary-aware Monte-Carlo intervals. Fits run mixed-model engines (glmmTMB, lme4) SEM engine (lavaan). Choosing ICC decision guide, choose_icc() helper, D-study projection rater counts (d_study()), autoplot() plots shipped. Bayesian engine roadmap; see project/MILESTONES.md.

code: library(intraclass)  icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, seed = 2024) #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 6 | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 24 of 24 cells (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(A,1)    0.290   [0.053, 0.715] #>   ICC(A,k)    0.620   [0.182, 0.910] #> Variance components: subject 2.556, rater 5.244, residual 1.019 #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1), ICC(A,k) = ICC(2,k) choose_icc(model = "twoway", type = "consistency", unit = "average", raters = "random") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: two-way random, consistency #> Recommendation: ICC(C,k) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Consistency: only the rank order must match; a constant per-rater offset is forgiven. #>   - Average: you will act on the mean of your raters. #>   - Random raters: a sample you generalize beyond, to the rater universe they were drawn from. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, type = "consistency", unit = "average") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly. set.seed(1) grid <- expand.grid(pupil = 1:5, classroom = 1:12, rater = 1:4) grid$score <- with(grid,   10 + rnorm(12, sd = 1.2)[classroom] +     rnorm(60, sd = 0.6)[(classroom - 1) * 5 + pupil] +     rnorm(4, sd = 0.4)[rater] + rnorm(nrow(grid), sd = 0.7)) school <- data.frame(   pupil = factor(paste(grid$classroom, grid$pupil, sep = "_")),   classroom = factor(grid$classroom),   rater = factor(grid$rater),   score = grid$score)  icc(school, score, subject = pupil, rater = rater, cluster = classroom, seed = 2024) #> ℹ Treating raters with the same label in different clusters as the same raters #>   (crossed with clusters, Design 1). #> ℹ If each cluster has its own raters, give them cluster-unique labels or pass #>   `design = "nested_in_clusters"`. #> # Intraclass correlation: multilevel two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 60 in 12 clusters | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 240 (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   level      index     estimate   95% CI #>   subject    ICC(A,1)    0.322   [0.162, 0.484] #>   subject    ICC(A,k)    0.655   [0.436, 0.790] #>   cluster    ICC(A,1)    0.870   [0.005, 0.973] #>   cluster    ICC(A,k)    0.964   [0.018, 0.993] #> Variance components: cluster 1.036, subject 0.305, rater 0.150, cluster:rater 0.005, residual 0.492 #>  #> This message is displayed once per session.

code: # Two-way absolute agreement, single rater, random raters (Shrout & Fleiss # ICC(2,1)): the two structural defaults (crossed, non-multilevel) are implied. choose_icc(type = "agreement", unit = "single", raters = "random") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Recommendation: ICC(A,1) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Absolute agreement: the value itself must match; a systematic difference between raters counts as error. #>   - Single rater: you will act on one rater's score. #>   - Random raters: a sample you generalize beyond, to the rater universe they were drawn from. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, unit = "single") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly.  # Consistency of the average of fixed raters -- McGraw & Wong ICC(C,k): choose_icc(type = "consistency", unit = "average", raters = "fixed") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: two-way mixed, consistency #> Recommendation: ICC(C,k) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(C,k) = ICC(3,k) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Consistency: only the rank order must match; a constant per-rater offset is forgiven. #>   - Average: you will act on the mean of your raters. #>   - Fixed raters: exactly these judges; the coefficient does not generalize past them. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, type = "consistency", raters = "fixed", unit = "average") #> Notes: #>   - Random raters is the recommended default for interrater reliability; use fixed only when these are the entire population of raters you will ever use. #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly.  # A one-way design (interchangeable raters): type/raters do not apply. choose_icc(model = "oneway", unit = "single") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: one-way random #> Recommendation: ICC(1) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(1) = ICC(1,1) #> Why: #>   - One-way: raters are interchangeable across subjects, so systematic rater differences are absorbed into error -- the most conservative ICC. #>   - Single rater: you will act on one rater's score. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, model = "oneway", unit = "single") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly.  # A multilevel design, both levels: choose_icc(type = "agreement", unit = "single", raters = "random",   multilevel = TRUE, level = "both") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: multilevel, two-way random, absolute agreement #> Recommendation: #>   subject: ICC(A,1) #>   cluster: ICC(A,1) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Absolute agreement: the value itself must match; a systematic difference between raters counts as error. #>   - Single rater: you will act on one rater's score. #>   - Random raters: a sample you generalize beyond, to the rater universe they were drawn from. #>   - Both levels: within-cluster (subject) and between-cluster (cluster) reliability side by side. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, cluster, unit = "single") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly. #>   - See vignette("multilevel-designs") for a worked multilevel example.

what: Which ICC is this, and when should you use it?
title: Intraclass correlation coefficient for a two-way design — autoplot.icc
text: Three choices pin coefficient: Agreement vs. consistency (type). Absolute agreement treats systematic differences raters (rater main effect, \(\sigma^2_r\)) error: use actual value matters raters must agree number (clinical scores, measurements). Consistency ignores constant per-rater offset: use relative standing matters. large gap two signals big systematic differences rater level – rating-procedure problem worth fixing. Single vs. average (unit). ICC(*,1) reliability single rater; ICC(*,k) reliability mean k raters. Report ICC(*,k) averaged score use. Random vs. fixed raters (raters). Random treats raters sample wish generalize beyond – recommended default interrater reliability. Fixed treats raters interest forgoes generalization; fit separately (raters fixed effects), balanced data matches random point estimate incomplete data genuinely differs. icc() warns choose . Fixed-rater consistency classic Shrout & Fleiss ICC(3,1).

title: Intraclass correlation coefficient for a two-way design — autoplot.icc
text: single rating per subject--rater cell, subject--rater interaction pure error separately identified; sum, residual variance \(\sigma^2_{res}\), estimable. Absolute agreement counts rater main effect \(\sigma^2_r\) error; consistency drops : $$ICC(,1) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + \sigma^2_r + \sigma^2_{res})$$ $$ICC(,k) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + (\sigma^2_r + \sigma^2_{res}) / k)$$ $$ICC(C,1) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + \sigma^2_{res})$$ $$ICC(C,k) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + \sigma^2_{res} / k)$$ \(\sigma^2_s\) subject (signal) variance k number raters.

what: intraclass: Modern Intraclass Correlation Coefficients — intraclass-package
title: intraclass: Modern Intraclass Correlation Coefficients — intraclass-package
text: Estimates interrater-reliability intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) within generalizability-theory framework using modern variance-component estimation (linear mixed models) rather classical analysis--variance mean-squares approach. Provides full ICC family (absolute agreement versus consistency, single versus average, fixed versus random raters, one-way versus two-way) boundary-aware Monte-Carlo confidence intervals, support imbalanced, incomplete, multilevel (nested) designs, decision-study projection numbers raters, interactive helper choosing correct coefficient. Multilevel methods follow ten Hove, Jorgensen van der Ark (2022) doi:10.1037/met0000391 .
